title: Aap or Tum
subtitle: Which 'you' fits this person?
question: How would you address this person?
tip: When in doubt, pick aap, it is never rude. Switch to tum once you are clearly friends or peers, or with younger family. The third level, tu, is only for real closeness, small children, or open anger, so as a learner you rarely need it.

left.label: AAP
bullets: Anyone older or senior: bosses, teachers, elders, officials.
Strangers and people you have only just met.
Formal and service settings: doctors, clerks, interviewers, customers.
The safe default. aap is never rude, so when unsure, choose it.

right.label: TUM
bullets: Close friends and people your own age you are comfortable with.
Younger family: little brothers, sisters, younger cousins.
Children and juniors you already know well.
Warm but still polite. The even more intimate tu is only for very close bonds, small kids, or anger.

items.explain: With anyone new to you, start with the respectful aap.
A senior at work always takes aap.
Teachers are shown respect, so aap.
Officials and authority figures take the respectful aap.
Anyone clearly older than you takes aap.
A stranger in a service setting takes aap.
In-laws are addressed with respect, so aap.
An interview is formal, so aap throughout.
A doctor is a professional you respect, so aap.
An older neighbour takes the respectful aap.
Formal office dealings take aap.
Elders, even a friend's, take aap.
Customers are addressed politely with aap.
A religious figure is shown respect, so aap.
A senior at work takes aap until they invite tum.
Professors take the respectful aap.

items.hint: the person at the bank desk
items.explain: A formal service setting takes aap.
Grandparents are honoured with aap.

items.prompt: an elderly woman on the bus
items.explain: An older stranger takes aap, doubly so for age.

items.hint: a visitor, not a close friend
items.explain: Guests are welcomed with the respectful aap.
Officials in formal settings take aap.
The more senior the person, the clearer the aap.
Close friends your age take the friendly tum.
Younger siblings take tum.
A younger sister takes the casual tum.
Old friends your age take tum.
Peers you study with take the friendly tum.
Cousins your age are family and peers, so tum.
A younger person you know well takes tum.

items.hint: a small kid in your care
items.explain: Children take tum (some families even use tu).
A close peer you share a room with takes tum.
Peers on your team take the friendly tum.
A younger junior you know well takes tum.
A peer you are friendly with takes tum.
Younger family members take tum.

items.prompt: a buddy you grew up with
items.explain: A lifelong friend your age takes tum.
Children take tum in friendly talk.
A young nephew takes the casual tum.
Friends your own age take tum.

items.prompt: a neighbour your age you joke with
items.explain: A peer you are comfortable with takes tum.
Someone younger you know well takes tum.
A young niece takes the casual tum.
College friends your age take tum.
Younger cousins take the friendly tum.
